temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913
temporal_spine.constitutional_context: U.S. Constitution ratification and Bill of Rights adoption

origin_interpretation: Late-18th-century constitutional meaning treated sovereignty as bounded popular authority, split between federal and state, with both limited by enumerated powers.
present_interpretation: Modern usage splits between state-issued money advocates, federal CBDC prohibition advocates, and private stablecoin promoters; all use 'sovereignty' while moving the actual exercise of power elsewhere.
semantic_shift: From bounded popular sovereignty → rhetorical sovereignty attached to private payment rails, state bullion depositories, or digital-dollar bans.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Constitutional sovereignty is popular sovereignty, bounded by enumerated powers. Modern monetary-sovereignty narratives redirect sovereign functions to private payment processors while retaining 'sovereignty' rhetoric.
legal_misdirect: State gold/silver legal tender bills and state bullion depositories invoke 'sovereignty' but create state-level CBDC-like rails. Federal CBDC ban plus private stablecoin promotion is described as 'sovereignty' while removing state issuance capability.
legal_position_delta: Origin: sovereign power is exercised by public institutions under law. Present: sovereign language is preserved while the exercise is delegated to private or proxy-state actors.
status_point: Both federal and state 'monetary sovereignty' actions expand state/parent-private control over money while claiming to reduce it. Conflict is structural, not partisan.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913
temporal_spine.constitutional_context: U.S. Constitution Article I, Section 8, Commerce Clause

origin_interpretation: Commerce meant exchange among communities and states; the Clause was aimed at preventing state trade barriers and discriminations.
present_interpretation: Commerce is treated as a general federal regulatory field covering money transmission, digital assets, and payment-system design.
semantic_shift: From specific interstate trade → all economic activity and payment-system architecture.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Commerce Clause was intended as a negative against state trade barriers, not as a general federal police power over all economic activity.
legal_misdirect: Modern Commerce Clause doctrine supports both CBDC bans and stablecoin promotion under the same clause, depending on political direction. The clause becomes a rubber stamp for either federal mandate or federal prohibition.
legal_position_delta: Origin: limited federal negative power. Present: broad federal positive power over money and digital assets.
status_point: Commerce Clause scope has expanded from trade barrier negation to all economic activity regulation.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: Militia was the whole people capable of arms, organized locally, trained collectively, and independent of central standing armies.
present_interpretation: Militia is treated as obsolete, ceremonial, or synonymous with National Guard under federal control.
semantic_shift: From universal citizen military readiness → selective, regulated, state-controlled exception.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Second Amendment prefatory clause ties arms right to well-regulated militia for free-state security. Modern law treats militia as National Guard or obsolete.
legal_misdirect: Assault-weapon bans, red-flag orders, and permit regimes reframe 'bear arms' as conditional privilege disconnected from militia-trained-band meaning. Historical fines/disarmament for loyalty crimes is ignored.
legal_position_delta: Origin: arms-bearing tied to collective free-state security. Present: arms-bearing treated as individualized privilege subject to administrative control.
status_point: Militia meaning has been inverted from citizen military readiness to regulated exclusion.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: Treason was understood as the gravest crime against the state itself, requiring intentional betrayal; the Framers narrowed it to prevent political weaponization.
present_interpretation: Treason is rarely charged as treason; functionally replaced by broader statutes with similar penalties.
semantic_shift: From narrow betrayal-of-state offense → broad national-security and ideological-prosecution toolkit.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Article III treason requires levying war or adhering to enemies, giving them aid and comfort; narrow, intentional definition.
legal_misdirect: Treason is rarely charged; adjacent statutes like seditious conspiracy, material support, espionage carry similar penalties without constitutional protections.
legal_position_delta: Origin: treason protected defendants from overcharge. Present: similar outcomes reached through wider statutes without the same procedural protections.
status_point: Treason text remains but functional prosecution shifted to broader statutory tools.

term: due process / law of the land

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: Due process meant general, public, pre-existing law; not arbitrary command, not retroactive deprivation, not secret adjudication.
present_interpretation: Due process is often procedural only; substantive claims are narrowed, financial/payment-system changes are treated as policy rather than rights deprivation.
semantic_shift: From general law safeguard → limited procedural check on administrative action.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments require due process of law before life, liberty, or property is taken.
legal_misdirect: CBDC ban, stablecoin delegation, and civil asset forfeiture operate without individualized due process for affected payment classes and asset holders.
legal_position_delta: Origin: law of the land shielded property/liberty from arbitrary reclassification. Present: payment-system redesign and asset-class reassignment can skip individualized process.
status_point: Due process is selectively applied; financial/payment-system changes bypass adversarial hearing.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: Warrants required particularity, oath, and judicial nexus to specific persons/places/things; search and seizure protections were personal-property safeguards.
present_interpretation: Warrant doctrine is hollowed out by NSLs, section 702, geofence warrants, administrative subpoenas, and bulk collection; probable cause is replaced by algorithmic risk scores.
semantic_shift: From particularized judicial warrant → generalized administrative and electronic suspicion.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Fourth Amendment requires particular warrants; modern surveillance substitutes programmatic authorization for particularized suspicion.
legal_misdirect: National security letters, section 702 collection, geofence warrants, and administrative subpoenas weaken particularity and judicial warrant requirement.
legal_position_delta: Origin: warrant clause protected persons from general searches. Present: search/ seizure can be administrative, bulk, and predictive.
status_point: Search and seizure protections are formally intact but practically narrowed.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: Punishments had to be proportionate, known in advance, and not torture-like; 'unusual' meant outside established common-law practice.
present_interpretation: Evolving-de standards doctrine allows judge-found 'unusual' labels against historical punishments; modern sentences can be expanded through enhancements and noncapital mandatory minimums.
semantic_shift: From historically bounded punishment concept → malleable contemporary judicial norm.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Eighth Amendment prohibits cruel and unusual punishments.
legal_misdirect: Punitive sentencing enhancements, solitary confinement duration, and noncapital mandatory minimums are contested under evolving standards.
legal_position_delta: Origin: fixed penalties and common-law continuity. Present: judge-determined evolving standards can expand punishment scope.
status_point: Eighth Amendment doctrine is now a moving target rather than a historical constraint.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: Bail and fines were to be calibrated to individual circumstances, not used as punitive substitutes where no individualized hearing occurred.
present_interpretation: Fixed civil fines, commercial disqualifications, and bail schedules override individualized excessiveness review.
semantic_shift: From individualized non-excessiveness safeguard → statutory flat-rate penalty tool.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Eighth Amendment excessive bail and excessive fines clauses.
legal_misdirect: Commercial driver-license disqualifications, civil fines, and bail schedules override individualized excessiveness review.
legal_position_delta: Origin: bail and fines required judicial calibration. Present: statutory and administrative schedules dominate.
status_point: Excessiveness review has become formal rather than substantive.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: Sixth Amendment rights were trial-centered: real counsel, live witnesses, face-to-face confrontation, and compulsory process against the state.
present_interpretation: Gag orders, sealed identities, classified evidence, ex parte submissions, and digital/remote testimony weaken or bypass these rights.
semantic_shift: From adversarial trial entitlement → managed disclosure regime favoring administrative and security exceptions.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Sixth Amendment guarantees assistance of counsel, compulsory process, confrontation, and witness rights.
legal_misdirect: Gag orders, sealed witness identities, classified evidence ex parte, and hearing-impaired compulsory-process failures.
legal_position_delta: Origin: defense-facing trial rights. Present: state-facing trial management often overrides defense process.
status_point: Trial rights remain formally but are functionally narrowed by classification and secrecy regimes.

term: assembly / petition / establishment / press / speech / religion

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: First Amendment cluster protected collective religious dissent, printed dissent, public assembly, and petition without prior license.
present_interpretation: Speech, assembly, and petition are heavily time-place-manner regulated; establishment is interpreted as non-endorsement rather than noninterference; press protections are weakened by secrecy and platform-carriage mandates.
semantic_shift: From pre-political rights of dissent → managed expressive activity subject to state licensing and criminal-tech wrappers.
constitutional_intent_conflict: First Amendment protects religious establishment noninterference, free exercise, speech, press, assembly, and petition.
legal_misdirect: Permit requirements for assembly, compelled speech frameworks, platform-carriage mandates, and foreign-influence registration regimes.
legal_position_delta: Origin: individual/collective dissent rights. Present: expressive activity regulated unless authorized.
status_point: First Amendment cluster is formally protected but administratively constrained.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: To bear arms meant to carry weapons in a manner connected with militia service and common defense.
present_interpretation: Bear arms is interpreted as individualized self-defense or sport, disconnected from collective militia purpose.
semantic_shift: From collective military readiness → individualized privilege conditioning.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Second Amendment protects individual right to keep and bear arms connected to militia security.
legal_misdirect: Assault-weapon bans, red-flag orders, and permit regimes reframe 'bear arms' as conditional privilege disconnected from militia-trained-band meaning.
legal_position_delta: Origin: militia-centered arms bearing. Present: permit-centered individual arms bearing.
status_point: Arms discourse is dominated by regulatory framing rather than militia-security framing.

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913

origin_interpretation: Amendment required deliberate supermajority process because constitutional meaning was treated as structured, fixed, and publicly known.
present_interpretation: Amendment by judicial interpretation, administrative practice, and evolving-standards doctrine bypasses Article V.
semantic_shift: From formal constitutional change → informal doctrinal evolution.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Article V amendment process requires supermajority state legislature or convention ratification.
legal_misdirect: Modern substantive due-process 'amendment by interpretation' functions as judicially created constitutional change.
legal_position_delta: Origin: formal amendment only. Present: informal doctrinal amendment common.
status_point: Article V is textually intact but functionally supplemented and often ignored.

term: executive / legislative / judicial / federal / state separation

temporal_spine.origin_source: Noah Webster, Dissertations on the English Language, Boston: Isaiah Thomas and Company, 1789
temporal_spine.modern_copy_source: Webster Revised Unabridged Dictionary, C. & G. Merriam Co., Springfield, Mass., 1913
temporal_spine.constitutional_context: U.S. Constitution Articles I-III and federalism structure

origin_interpretation: Separation of powers was structural: legislature makes law, executive enforces, judiciary interprets; federal and state spheres were distinct.
present_interpretation: Administrative agencies fuse legislative, executive, and judicial functions; federal power routinely preempts state police powers.
semantic_shift: From separated branches and dual sovereignty → blended agency power with federal supremacy.
constitutional_intent_conflict: Separation of powers among legislature, executive, and judiciary; federalism balance between national and state governments.
legal_misdirect: Administrative state combines legislative, executive, and judicial functions in agencies.
legal_position_delta: Origin: separated powers and dual sovereignty. Present: administrative fusion and federal dominance.
status_point: Nondelegation doctrine is asserted but rarely enforced; agency rulemaking is functionally legislation.
